title: Kaitiakitanga in Action: Guardianship of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Te Mahi Kaitiaki: Te Tiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Students will explore kaitiakitanga (guardianship) through the lens of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream), a significant waterway flowing through Ngāti Kahungunu rohe. They will learn about the traditional practices of monitoring and protecting the awa, the historical relationship between local hapū and this water source, and contemporary challenges facing the stream including urban runoff and water quality issues.
learningObjectives: Identify and explain at least three traditional Ngāti Kahungunu practices for monitoring and protecting waterways
Analyze current water quality data from Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and identify one environmental concern
Collaboratively design a practical kaitiakitanga action plan to address an identified issue affecting the awa
Present findings and recommendations using appropriate te reo Māori terms and cultural protocols

activities.title: Whakapapa of the Awa: Understanding Our Connection
activities.description: Students begin with a karakia and explore the significance of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku through local pūrākau (stories) and maps. They create visual representations showing the awa's journey through our community and its importance to Ngāti Kahungunu identity and survival.

activities.title: Wai Māori, Wai Ora: Traditional Monitoring Practices
activities.description: Working with a local kaumatua, students learn traditional methods for assessing water health including observing insect life (ngā pūwai), water clarity, and native vegetation. They practice these assessment techniques at a safe access point along the awa.

activities.title: He Raru, He Rongoā: Investigating Modern Challenges
activities.description: Students analyze recent water quality data from Hawke's Bay Regional Council for Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. They identify patterns, compare data with traditional indicators, and investigate potential sources of pollution through mapping exercises.

activities.title: Manaaki Whenua, Manaaki Tangata: Action Planning
activities.description: In small groups, students develop practical kaitiakitanga action plans addressing one identified issue. Plans must incorporate both mātauranga Māori and scientific approaches, considering feasibility and potential impact on the community.

activities.title: Whakamana Te Awa: Sharing Our Kaitiakitanga
activities.description: Groups present their action plans to classmates and invited community members using appropriate pepeha and mihimihi. They create visual displays or digital presentations that honor both traditional knowledge and contemporary solutions.

resources: Kaumatua Hone Smith (local Ngāti Kahungunu elder and water quality knowledge holder)
Hawke's Bay Regional Council's 'State of the Environment' water quality data for Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Traditional water testing kit and modern water quality testing strips
'Te Mana o te Wai' educational resources from Ministry for the Environment

tasks: Create an annotated map showing the journey of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and its significance to local hapū
Complete a water quality monitoring journal comparing traditional and scientific assessment methods
Develop and present a kaitiakitanga action plan addressing a specific environmental concern
Participate in a reflective discussion about personal and collective responsibilities to the awa

nzcAlignment: Social Studies L4: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
Social Studies L4: Understand how the ways in which leadership of groups is acquired and exercised have consequences for communities and societies
Social Studies L4: Understand how people manage natural and physical resources
Health and Physical Education L4: Demonstrate understanding of factors that contribute to personal identity and celebrate individuality and affirm diversity
